summary: Jeff Atwood argues that URL shortening services, popularized by Twitter's 140-character limit, pose a serious threat to the web's fundamental architecture. He explains how Twitter's refusal to implement basic hyperlink markup forced users to adopt third-party URL shorteners, which have evolved from simple utilities into vehicles for tracking, advertising, and content manipulation. Atwood contends that these services insert themselves as intermediaries in the web's hyperlink system, potentially undermining PageRank and the open web. He suggests that if URL shortening must exist, it should be handled by search engines who already index all URLs rather than opportunistic startups building businesses on web infrastructure fragmentation.
key_points: Twitter's technical decision to not support hyperlink markup forced mainstream adoption of URL shortening services
URL shorteners add a fragile layer of indirection to the web, creating new points of failure assembled from 'unvetted PHP and MySQL'
Modern URL shorteners have evolved from utilities into tools for tracking, advertising, framing content, and monetizing clicks
The hyperlink is the foundation of PageRank and the web itself, making URL shortener intermediaries fundamentally dangerous
Search engines would be the logical providers of URL shortening since they already cache every URL
money_quotes: The humble hyperlink, thanks to pervasive URL shortening, can now be wielded as a weapon.
Every tiny URL is another baby step towards destroying the web as we know it.
The current situation is completely accidental.
URL shorteners are no longer viewed as utility services to make URLs more convenient, but a way to subjugate, control, and perhaps worst of all, 'monetize' the web experience.
The internet is the house that PageRank built, and it's all predicated on hyperlinks.
As a software developer, you'll be fortunate to build one project that achieves critical mass in your entire life.
Twitter, I can assure you, is far more brain damaged than you can possibly imagine.
key_insight: Twitter's arbitrary technical constraints accidentally created a thriving ecosystem of URL shortening parasites that threaten the web's fundamental architecture of transparent, direct hyperlinks.
